title: Traveling with Kyle - Living in Chile part 9/17

transcript.text: Yeah, anyways, um, so I was there for four months, um, working on the house until, like, I guess, the woman who I was working, who had kind of the institute, the woman who was paying me to do classes for her, I guess, working for this company that made toilet paper that was owned by the Germans.

Just outside of town, like, I worked for her, but she kept trying to shortchange me because she, like, I don't know if it was on purpose or what, but she would have, she would keep track of her, of my hours on a, uh, on a, on a small notepad, like with a pencil pencil.
So every month she'd be like, yeah, I don't use 250,000 pit mil pesos.
And I'm like, what?
No, you owe me this 400,000.
She's like, what?
No, I don't.
I need this much.
And I'm like, all right, well, look, cause I kept track on my little computer.
I had a little, uh, what are those called?
A little palm.
I'm like, see, look, this is the day I work.
It was the day I work.
And I added up and she's like, Oh God, you win.
Okay.
And so pay me.
But, uh, but like one day, but, but then she'd pay me late.
And so that pissed off my wife.
And like one night, my wife got really pissed off and got really drunk and calls her.
Oh, and, but my wife had me, had me get the license plate to her car first.
So she got her license plate, found out when she bought the car, what her first name is,
What her last name is, where she lives, all this information.
So she leaves a message on her phone, like totally drunk and screaming, you, a license plate.
So-and-so live at this address.
So-and-so if you don't pay Kyle, we're going to sue your ass, you know, you fucking bitch.
And like hangs up.
And so, and then like the lady, she never said anything to me, but I think it pissed her
Off because she ended up firing me.
Um, and the reason that she said it was kind of funny because like, uh, she like, I was
Doing this one class on the edge of town and she called me up right at the end of the
Classes.
You go, Oh yeah, I need you to take this class.
And I'm like, yeah, but it takes me 50 minutes to get there.
And the class starts in a half hour and she's like, Oh, don't worry about that.
Just show up late.
So I go, okay.
So I go to the class and I do the class.
And then the next day she goes, Hey, your students said you should have all sweaty.
And so no more classes for you.
But you know, I should have sweaty because I was 20 minutes late.
But anyways, so I was thinking, okay, well, I'll just go home.
I haven't been home for four years, two and a half months.
I've been out of the country.
I'm going to go home and visit my mom.
And, uh, since I'm not working and then when I come back, I'll just be, I'll just get
Work for myself.
So, uh, I bought a ticket and then they had, so then our family decided they were going
To have a going away party for me.
Um, but they, uh, they had me pay, buy all the booze, pay for all the booze, which is
Like a couple hundred bucks worth of booze.
They turned it into this big party for all their friends and everything.
And, uh, I was kind of, kind of grumpy at the time because the whole time that I built
A house that I was worked for the four months that I was there, re totally revamping their
House painted a hundred feet of wall on one side, 30 feet of wall on the other.
Get rid of 25 pickup loads full of trash.
Hundred, hundred pound, 50, hundred pound bags of cement filling up holes in their house
Before I painted it.
Repaved the whole part of the house in the back between her mom's house and her house.
And I got like three, two giant pickup, like truckloads, like six, two, six foot high piles
Of, uh, these, these rocks that are like this big to put all over the area where they park
Cars because it was all dirt and it would be cool.
Like when it rained and the water would just go under and you can walk around without tracking
Mud around and getting rid of two cars that had been stuck back there.
The whole time that this happened, even though in this house, the people who live in this house,
It's like the main house, which is, Oh no, I'll just keep going, which is two bedrooms.
Um, which is a two bedroom house with the, with the, with the bathroom, a kitchen, a living
Room.
And then right behind is my, my wife's house, which is, uh, basically when I moved in, it
Was one bedroom, one small living room connected to a kitchen, but there's no bathroom because
The bathroom was, so it was one bathroom and then two structures.
But there in the back of my wife and her son and me.
And then the main house lived my wife's father, her mother, her brother.
Oh, Oh yeah.
And then there was a little structure in between, which is like a tiny room with the bunk bed
And then another tiny room with his room.
And then he had his own kitchen with water.
But yes, it was him, his wife and his two kids.
And then my wife's parents, her brother.
And then this other kid who they adopted because he comes from a family of like 13.
His real mother has like 13 kids and wasn't able to, uh, he was basically an orphan.
So they adopted him.
Um, so there was 13 of us living in one, one house with one bathroom.
And, uh, but anyways, the whole time I was there, I guess her brother was having trouble.
He was, he, he, he kind of goes in and out of a crack binges, which I guess crack.
It's not really crack, like American crack.
It's like pasta base paste.
It's, it's the stuff that it's like yellow colored crystalline thing.
Um, it's like the goop that falls along the side of the cooking platter when you make cocaine.
And it was really, really cheap to buy.
It's like 500 pesos, which was like 75 cents at the time I was there.
And you get like enough to, to smoke like maybe a half dozen hits out of it.
So it's really cheap.
Um, but each time you take an inhalation, it's like,
It's, it has a similar feeling of like free base cocaine.
Only not, not quite as strong and definitely not as good.
Like a lot more unhealthy.
And he would have these binges where he would like, um, steal the family's money.
Like one time, I guess he took the life savings of 500 bucks and disappeared for five days smoking it.
And then another time he, um, he disappeared.
He, and when I was there, he stole all of the appliances out of the living room.
And so then they had to, um, to actually lock up their main bedroom and the parents did.
And I had to, we had to keep our house padlocked.
Like if I go to the bathroom, I'd have to padlock my house because you know, that's what my wife said I would have to do.
Or else you go in and steal something.
And I guess he had stolen money from, from her in the past, like a large amount of money before.
And so that's why the father of her kid like isn't friends.
Cause I guess he was living there.
Um, so anyway, he was going through one of these binges.
And so he wasn't really wanting to help me.
Like whenever I'd start to work on the house, he would like disappear.
And then I'd walk around to go get some at the hardware store and he'd be sitting on the corner on the, on the, on the sidewalk, just sitting there.
Uh, so he basically avoided me as soon as I start working.
And then the other brother who lived there, he was a really cool kid, really good freestyle rapper, but he was lazy.
He helped me for like five minutes once, but he was working really slow and only did a patch of like this big patch against bricks.
One afternoon, his friends came over and helped to paint the part of the house, but that's it.
That's the only hope I got in four months.
Oh, and I also in that four months, I finished building this other house that they made that this, that this guy made.
Um, he, uh, he, uh, yeah, he didn't finish it.
So I went in there and I, and I paved the rest of the house, which was two, which was basically one room divided by this little divider.
It was like this wide.
Um, and I plugged up the holes that were between the roof and the top of the wall with, with, you know, cardboard board, which is what the house will build that up.
Filled in all of the holes with the, uh, of the, uh, of the bricks and then painted it, the whole house inside and out and paved a big patio on the front part of the house.
And put a door in it on the house.
So I did that.
It took me four months and I didn't have any help the whole time.
One afternoon for a couple hours.
They were helping me with actually building that one house, plugging in like cement and stuff.
Oh yeah.
Cause I put in two windows.
I bought the window frames to slide into the window and then patched it up, you know, with, with cement and stuff.
And so they helped me for a couple hours there, but that's the only help I had just like one afternoon in four months.
So I was like, kind of like.
